articles.title: 1. Player Introduction
articles.content:
[h1]The Story So Far[/h1]
[aloud]Having completed your early training as a [mark]<insert your class here>[/mark], you were left at loose ends when your master kicked you out and said to "go make a living with what I taught you". After knocking about for a while, you found yourself in the Free City of Haven, not sure what to do. Spying a recruiting poster for the Haven City Watch , you thought "what the hell, at least the training will kill a few weeks."
[/aloud]
What's that you say? It didn't happen that way? Then perhaps you can tell me how you ended up in Haven and attending The Academy. Go ahead, I'll wait. 

[mark]<insert your story here>[/mark]

OK, now that we've got that straight...

[aloud]
The training was pretty straightforward but at the same time not what you really expected. Instead of learning how to stop crime and keep people safe, it was more about marching, discipline, and umpteen different ways to say "Oy! What's going on here, then?" While you did learn about the structure of the watch and how it functions in Haven, and how the city government and legal system work, you were somewhat disappointed to learn that the role was more about keeping drunks in line and stopping fights that break out - not really about helping people. Whenever you tried to point this out you were told to shut it and get back in line. On the plus side you managed to find a handful of kindred spirits who were equally disillusioned. Over several evenings at the local tavern and countless hours of pointless scutwork at The Academy you became fast friends.
[/aloud]

Now what? There are more stories to tell about how you all came together? Do tell! 

[mark]<Share how your character might be connected to other party member, aside from being fellow Trainees>[/mark]

[aloud]
After six seemingly pointless weeks, graduation day rolled around. The ceremony was pretty basic; you got your badge, a truncheon, a bell to call for help, handshakes from Academy Captain Themo Lundek and from Mikyl Swiftsword, the Commander of the Watch. From there you wandered over the posting board to see where you would start your career as Watchman. While other Trainee names were listed with a station house, your name only had a cryptic "See Acting Captain Lyza , The Giggling Gryphon, tomorrow at 9:00am." Even odder is that you've never heard of The Giggling Gryphon, it's certainly not one of the local Academy hangouts. On the plus side, your friends all had the same message. Oh well, since you have nothing to do until tomorrow at 9am you decide to join the rest of your classmates for a drink (shocking, I know).
[/aloud]

While out with your classmates, you managed to learn the following: 
[ul]
[li]The Giggling Gryphon  is somewhere on a backstreet in The Commons, off Loom St. 
[li]Lyza was a rising star, recently promoted from Station Officer to Acting Captain - whatever that is. The normal promotion from Station Officer comes with a station to command but she didn't get one so people are starting to suspect maybe she screwed up somehow. 
[li]There are rumours of an underground trade in slaves, something not heard of in many years. 
[li]There are rumours that some high mucky-muck on the Council of Boroughs is meddling in the Watch and Watch Commander Swiftsword isn't happy. 
[li]A rival group seems to be making a run at the Thieves' Guild. 
[li]An heir to the Kingdom of Haven has been found and is riding to Haven with an army. Nobody gives this much credence however given there hasn't been a king here for centuries. 
[li]Did you hear about the ship that was lost at sea, The Nightmare? They say that it was carrying a fortune in gold and jewels now everyone is looking for the wreckage.
[/ul]
[aloud]
It is now 6:00am and reveille is sounding at The Academy barracks where you've been staying. You have three hours to get up, dressed, fed and find The Giggling Gryphon somewhere on the other side of the river. Oh, and you have a splitting headache and feel slightly nauseous from too much...whatever that was you had last night.
[/aloud]

articles.title: 2. Player Options
articles.content:
[h2]Playable Classes[/h2]
The following classes are available for players in this campaign. 
[row]
[col3]
[ul]
[li]@[Barbarian](article:e88e37b5-9366-417e-b6db-3ae4c651da8b)
    [li]@[Cleric](article:d01be7d4-90e1-41c2-a4b5-c17e64482245)
    [li]@[Fighter](article:a58dd1e5-19cf-43c1-89dd-6db2f8bcb266)
    [li]@[Paladin](article:09c9de18-ae69-4e82-bc4c-91110ec6f619)
    [li]@[Physician](article:cf8f9e0f-0354-46c2-9829-479a14ac4fde)
[/ul]
[/col3]
[col3]
[ul]
[li]@[Ranger](article:bda8e44d-596b-4e4a-9b32-a458d12fca8b)
    [li]@[Rogue](article:21ec75f4-dce9-4848-8a5e-64380aebff6a)
    [li]@[Sorcerer](article:c139d392-0d96-4f64-858a-f60d05a53ef4)
    [li]@[Warlock](article:5141ca6e-29f1-492f-87f7-f3c397f5d205)
    [li]@[Wizard](article:20ee6b49-786b-4eae-b4dd-394bfdf3aedd)
[/ul]
[/col3]
[col3]
[/col3]
[/row]
Note: Don't worry if nobody wants to be a healer, I have an NPC Physician ready to step in.

[h2]Playable Races[/h2]
The following races are available for players in this campaign:
[row]
	[col3]
  	[ul]
			[li]@[Dwarf](ethnicity:bcb7c996-cab3-4450-bfab-484289ea1ce6)
			[li]@[Elf](ethnicity:8594e1ec-8faf-415d-930e-771122a32ec5) (excluding Drow)
 	    [li]@[Gnome](ethnicity:2aac53c9-a827-44c5-8493-039c79ccf6dd)
 	    [li]@[Halfling](ethnicity:8c88a60a-8360-427a-b1d8-ed7b95de6cf8)
		[/ul]
	[/col3]
	[col3]
  	[ul]
  [li]@[Half-Elf](ethnicity:50625ce1-dd61-4ca4-93f9-c7b04477dbaf)
      [li]@[Half-Orc](ethnicity:ab24d884-4c35-4e98-b7ea-88be7b776a96)
      [li]@[Human](ethnicity:f50a062c-e8a5-4e11-a88c-b1cad339e530)
      [li]@[Tiefling](ethnicity:5f1317af-a708-4c18-be36-ed66129c7865)
    [/ul]
	[/col3]
  [col3]
  [/col3]
[/row]

There are other races in Haven: @[Avanthar](ethnicity:10367593-b9d9-4dca-94f3-7f44bf047ebb), @[Centaur](ethnicity:4dd47c1d-a09d-4705-ac61-d97120519c51), @[Goliath](ethnicity:5f5c5202-5382-47ee-8488-f9fe54300998), Goblin, Troll and the odd Pixie.

[h2]Alignment[/h2]
Since you are all members of the City Watch, the following alignments are fine.[br]
[row]
  [col3]
  	[ul]
  [li]Lawful Good
			[li]Lawful Neutral
		[/ul]
  [/col3]
  [col3]
  	[ul]
  [li]Neutral Good
			[li]Neutral
		[/ul]
  [/col3]
  [col3]
  [/col3]
[/row]
[br]
If you can make a case for another alignment that doesn't involve somehow shafting the party or another player I'll consider it.

[h2]Spells[/h2]
I'll be reviewing the spells to see if there's anything I foresee as world wrecking but don't expect to find any.  Also note that the @[Physician](article:cf8f9e0f-0354-46c2-9829-479a14ac4fde) has two class-specific spells.

[h2]Leveling Up[/h2]
I expect to be using the milestone method of leveling, at least initially. This means that you will gain a level at set point in the plot. However, I don't know yet how this will work out if you choose to ignore my plotting and follow your own ;-)

[h2]Reference Material[/h2]
- Players Handbook
- Xanathar's Guide to Everything
- Tasha's Cauldron of Everything

articles.title: 3. Optional and House Rules
articles.content:
[h1]Rule #1: Don't be a Dick[/h1]
Party members must work with the party unless the other players are cool with it for the sake of roleplay. Cooperation is defined negatively, through a list of examples of non-cooperation. Forms of non-cooperation include: 
[ul]
[li]Stealing from party members (includes not sharing loot).
[li]Hiding during a fight because your character is “cowardly” and feels no loyalty to the party.
[li]Attacking someone while most of the party want to negotiate, effectively forcing the party to do what you want and fight. (“I am a barbarian and I have no patience” isn’t a valid excuse.)
[li]Refusing to take prisoners when that’s what a majority want.
[li]Abusing the norm against no PvP by putting the party in a situation where they must choose between attacking you, letting you die alone or joining in an activity they really don’t want to (e. g. attacking the town guards).
[li]Doing things that would be repugnant to the group’s morality, e.g., torture for fun. Especially if you act shocked when the other players call you on it, in or out of game.
[/ul]
Additionally, when a player does these things, especially when they do them consistently in a way that isn’t fun, the GM shouldn’t expect them to solve it in game. An over the table conversation is necessary. In extreme cases the GM might even be justified in vetoing an action (“I use sleight of hand to steal that players magic ring.” “No, you don’t”.) Generally though it’s possible to prevent it from coming to this point. 


[h1]Character Generation and Leveling[/h1]
[h2]Rolling for Stating Attribute Scores[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] 
[ol]
[li]Do six rolls of 4d6 (re-rolling 1s). Drop the lowest die to get 6 numbers from 6-18. GM must witness this step.
[li]Add up the 6 number. If the total is less than 70, you can choose to throw them out and go back to step 1.
[li]Determine the attribute modifiers provided by these rolls (+ve and -ve mods). If the total exceeds 8, throw them out and go back to step 1.
[li]Arrange the stat as desired from these "rolls".
[li]Add any racial stat modifiers.
[/ol]
Alternatively, you can skip steps 1 to 3 if you send me a number from 1 - 100. I'll give you a set of "rolls" that works. Yes, I generated 100 sets of 6 rolls that fit the criteria :-)

[b]Explanation:[/b] Nobody wants to play a weak character, after all, we are adventures. This method allows for decent stats and the possibility of a low stat (weakness), which is incredibly fun for roleplay purposes. That is something the point-buy system doesn't lean into. However, this system has the potential of making really strong early game characters. That is why a limit of a total of +8 modifiers is present. +8 may seem underwhelming but compared to the max of +7 the Point-Buy system allows, the limit is reasonable. 

[h2]Feats[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] During character creation, a player may elect to swap one (and only one) Attribute Score bonus and instead acquire one Feat. For example, half-elves get +2 to Charisma; instead, a player may gain +1 CHA and take a feat.  Refer to @[Feats](article:e67305ff-5b1d-4997-a08b-66b38fb85353) for what's available. Before taking a feat, you must obtain the GM's permission as may veto a choice but must do so when it is first chosen, allowing the player to select a different one. Any feat that includes a plus to any of the character's attribute scores will be vetoed at character creation time.

[b]Explanation:[/b] Feats add flavour but can also make for overpowered characters.

[h2]Racial Modifiers[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] Per Tasha's Cauldron of Everything (one of the expansion books), you may choose to apply racial attribute modifiers to different attributes [i]if it fits your character description[/i]. For example, if your race normally gets +2 Strength and +1 Constitution but you want to be a weakling performer you could apply the +2 to Charisma and +1 to Dexterity. 
[b]Please review your proposal with the GM before proceeding.[/b]

[b]Explanation:[/b] Gives you more choice to adapt your stats to the character.

[h2]Alignment is a Guideline, not a Prison[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] Players are not restricted to certain actions due to their alignment. However, if your character has a certain devotion or deity that their abilities come from, for example, paladin, cleric, etc. the GM will determine the consequences (if any) for your actions. Just as stats change, alignment may change throughout the course of the campaign and is not set in stone. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] This rule is to avoid stubborn characters who refuse to do a certain action due to alignment, putting the party at a hindrance. They are not obligated to do, or not do, actions because of their alignment, however of course they still may wish to do whatever they choose so. 

[h2]Multiclassing[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] Multiclassing is an optional rule that I'd prefer not to use. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] Multiclassing can slow the game pace as players need to keep two sets of abilities in mind and favors some classes over others. It also has the potential to create extreme metagaming. 

[h2]One Character per Player[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] Each player may have one and only one character.  This may be waived in exceptional circumstances and for limited durations.

[b]Explanation:[/b] I've played multiple characters and seen it done and it simply takes too much time and slows things down, especially combat.

[h2]Leveling Up: Feats[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] As per PHB, any time you level up and gain an Ability Score Improvement, you may instead choose a feat for which you meet the prerequisites. Refer to this document for the feats from the three main source books. Before taking a feat, you must obtain the GM's permission as may veto a choice but must do so when it is first chosen, allowing the player to select a different one. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] Feats add flavour but can also make for overpowered characters.

[h2]Leveling Up: New HP[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] When a player levels up, they may choose either of the following:
[ul]
[li]Roll their Hit Die then choose that roll (always reroll a 1) or scrap it for the GM’s mystery roll. Then add their Constitution modifier.
[li]Choose half their Hit Die rounded down and add their Constitution modifier (e.g., if you use d8 for hit die take 4 + CON modifier)
[/ul]
[b]Explanation:[/b] Hit Dice/HP are very important when it comes to a character. It may determine whether they live or die. When it comes to rolling for HP, statistics show that this is the most cheated stat players roll on. A constant low roll could result in a fragile character. This may lead to the player feeling dissatisfied with their character throughout the game. This may also lead to strange outcomes of a wizard tank and a pushover barbarian. While this may be fun for some players in the roleplay light. Not all players enjoy having their most important stat based on random chance. 

With this rule, players can choose whether they want to risk and roll for a higher health pool with possible chances of a higher result or low result. Or take the slightly above average and dependant route of a static number. This choice is entirely up to the player, and they may change their option every time they role for new HP/Hit Die. However, to still encourage rolls, the player may roll their Hit Die and the GM rolls a mystery Hit die for them at the same time. The player may then choose their roll or to take the GM’s mystery roll. There will be no takebacks if the player chooses GM mystery roll.

What this does is allow a lot of freedom during Hit Die level up. Letting the player feel satisfied with any choice they make. Also, everyone hates getting a 1 on the roll. Let's reroll them! 

[h2]A Reason to Be Here[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] Ensure your character has a reason to be in and want to stay in the party.

[b]Explanation:[/b] [url:https://www.youtube.com/shorts/cL7u6ujniI0|tab]See this short video[/url] (opens a new tab)


[h1]Combat[/h1]
[h2]Nat 20 to Hit[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] At Nat 20 is a critical hit only if you do in fact hit the target (including other modifiers). For example, a Nat 20 at +4 to hit misses if the target has AC 25. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] Automatic hits with much larger damage 5% of the time is just too much. 

[h2]Nat 1 to Hit[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] This is just a really bad miss. We won't be using a fumble/critical miss table. Unless you really want to, I do have fumble tables. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] Prioritizing speed of combat over fumble effect. I can be convinced to go the other way. 

[h2]Critical Hit Damage[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] We will use the "Perkins Crit" method for determining damage on a critical hit. Using the by-the-book method, damage dice are rolled twice, added together and then your damage modifier is added. The Perkins method assumes that one roll will be max value, so you only roll the other one. Then add damage modifiers as normal.

[b]Explanation:[/b] Using this method ensures that the value of a crit can't be cancelled out by a bad damage roll. But don't forget that your enemies will use the same method! 

[h2]Time Limited Turns[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] The GM looks at the player whose turn it is and starts counting down from a number (or maybe uses a timer app?). If the player takes no action by the time the count reaches zero, the GM skips that players turn. Their character froze up in the moment and took the “Dodge Action”. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] Two factors are at play here.

Real combat is chaotic and there is no time to stop and have conversations with others about what you should do. The countdown will allow for brief, shouted suggestions from other characters but require you to make a quick decision.

Combat can seem to take forever so we want to speed things up. Players should be planning their next move while waiting for their turn - the countdown will allow for some adjustment based on what just happened.

Note: Some leeway is granted for players still learning or who have a mechanical question. We don't want to frustrate players still learning the action economy or class abilities. 

[h2]Flanking, or Get Creative to Get Advantage[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] The optional flanking will not be used. Instead, get creative in describing what you do (i.e., impress, amuse the GM and other players) and the GM may give you advantage. For example, a player describes that they swing across a chandelier passing an acrobatics check with flying colors to attack the bad guy mid-swing. Yeah, that gets advantage. Other cool moves and their respected skill checks will matter based on the circumstances. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] The flanking rule is optional...and mechanical and boring. I'd rather be entertained. 

[s]
[h2]Enemy Initiative Order[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] Enemy initiative is determined by rolling 1dx (where x = number of players + 1). Enemies are slotted individually between the player turns starting with the result of the die roll.

[container:indent-40]
Example: 5 players vs. 8 Kobolds
[ol]
[li]GM rolls a d6 (# players + 1) and gets a 3
[li]Initiative order will be:
[li]highest player initiative
[li]2nd highest player
[li]first kobold
[li]3rd player
[li]2nd kobold
[li]etc.
[/ol]
Remaining kobolds go after the last player.
[/container]

[b]Explanation:[/b] The goal of this rule is to make combat go Player, Enemy, Player, Enemy, etc. Setting up this combat order is faster because the GM ignores rolling for initiative and spending time on math. Having enemies go alternate with players makes the combat feel more dynamic, flexible, and reactionary. Players have a chance to react to a status effect or a turn coming up. This also avoids clusters of enemies attacking a specific player at once or the party not being able to help because their turns are so far behind. 
[/s]

[h2]Death Saves[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] Death saves will be rolled in private with only the player and GM knowing the result, which may NOT be communicated to the other players. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] Add tension in deciding when to heal your fallen comrade. It also avoids healer metagaming because they won't know if they can wait another turn or two. 

[h2]The “Last Breath” Option[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] If a character rolls their final death saving throw and fails (or otherwise dies) they have the option of choosing a “Last Breath” or dying as normal. If they elect “Last Breath” then the character gets up using their last bit of energy giving themselves one final turn. The only restriction on this action is that it cannot lead to being healed in any manner. Any ability check and attack rolled during this turn by that player has advantage. Once the Last Breath turn is over, the player dies, and they cannot be resurrected by ANY means. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] There are many reasons why this rule is in here. If a player wants to die, they have the option of choosing this to attempt to go out in a blaze of glory. Thus, if the party has no resurrection abilities and the player does not want to risk them finding a way to revive them, (if there even is any) they can go out in style. This rule makes the player’s death meaningful and can lead to some great stories. This also lets the player sacrifice themselves to save the party. 


[h1]Spells and Casting[/h1]
[h2]Mage Armour[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] uses the higher of the target's Dex modifier, or casters primary spell ability modifier. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] Better armour for casters. 

[h2]Spell Components[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] 
[ol]
[li]No spell pouch (or equivalent) means you don't have the materials.
[li]It is assumed that your character has the *simple* components to cast your spells in their inventory. 
[li]For spells that require a *set* amount of gold or *specific* (semi-)precious stones, the caster must have them on their person. If in doubt, ask the GM!
[li]For spells requiring materials worth up to a certain gold amount such as gems, or other materials of value, you can substitute  the equivalent amount of gold. You must have that amount of money on you.
[/ol]

[b]Explanation:[/b] The player's handbook pretty much states the same rule, but just to clarify why this is here. There are some powerful spells in D&D, they have a cost to using them on purpose. That way a caster will not go casting a strong spell left and right. If a spell has a requirement of a set amount of gold that needs to be used, gem or diamond. It will be the player's responsibility to acquire and manage these materials. 

[h2]Opportunity Spells[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] When a hostile creature’s movement provokes an opportunity attack from a player, the player may instead cast an "opportunity spell". The spell must have a casting time of 1 action, be of level 2 or lower, and must target only that creature. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] This makes opportunity attacks more exciting because the player now has more choices on what they would like to do. This also gives a heavy caster focused build more flexibility instead of punishing them for not having a high DEX/STR score forcing them to use a melee weapon. 

[h2]Cast before Effect[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] The casting of a spell is completed before the effect takes place. This means the Verbal, Somatic, Material components are "used" before the effect happens. This applies unless explicitly stated otherwise in the spell description. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] Needed to clarify some spells where the effect *could be* considered to be part of the casting. For example, in a Suggestion spell, what is being suggested is not the Verbal component of the spell. Perhaps the Verbal component is an arcane translation of "I suggest that you...". More importantly, someone can see that a spell is being cast *before* the effect, not in parallel with the effect. 

[h2]Spell Clarifications[/h2]
The following clarifications/rulings will apply. Others may be added as needed.
[ul]
[li][u]Acid Splash:[/u] May only be cast on a creature. Acid Splash does not damage objects.
[li][u]Banishment:[/u] Must have an item the target is afraid of or wants to get away from.
[li][u]Contagion:[/u] Disease effect is chosen on hit, but effect starts only after 3 failed saving throws.
[li][u]Counterspell:[/u] Must be able to see the caster and you will not know what spell the caster is casting.
[li][u]Mage Hand:[/u] This spell as stated cannot be used to directly give a player advantage on a roll. It may however be used to do something that in turn leads to a player having advantage.
[li][u]Message:[/u] The V component is required to cast the spell, which is done before the spell takes effect. Thus, the message you send is NOT the verbal component.
[li][u]Phantasmal Force:[/u] Does not let you control or move the illusion after it is created.
[li][u]Sending:[/u] The V component is required to cast the spell, which is done before the spell takes effect. Thus, the message you send is NOT the verbal component.
[li][u]Suggestion:[/u] The V component is required to cast the spell, which is done before the spell takes effect. Thus, the message you send is NOT the verbal component.
[/ul]

And a reminder: if the spell has Verbal or Somatic components, other creatures may be able to see or hear you casting.

[h2]More on Suggestion[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] The spell description is reworded to be
[container:indent-40]
You suggest a course of activity (limited to a sentence or two) and magically influence a creature you can see within range that can hear and understand you. 

Creatures that can't be charmed are immune to this effect. The suggestion must be worded in such a manner as to make the course of action sound reasonable. A reasonable suggestion is something that a creature could be persuaded, deceived, or intimidated to do with a high enough Charisma check even without magic. If the suggestion is worded in such a way that the DC for the Charisma check would be higher than 30, the spell ends.

Asking the creature to stab itself, throw itself onto a spear, immolate itself, or do some other obviously harmful act also ends the spell.

The target must make a Wisdom saving throw. On a failed save, it pursues the course of action you described to the best of its ability. The suggested course of action can continue for the entire duration. If the suggested activity can be completed in a shorter time, the spell ends when the subject finishes what it was asked to do. You can also specify conditions that will trigger a special activity during the duration. For example, you might suggest that a knight give her warhorse to the first beggar she meets. If the condition isn't met before the spell expires, the activity isn't performed.

If you or any of your companions damage the target, the spell ends.
[/container]

[b]Explanation:[/b] The existing wording is far from clear and leads to far too strong a result for a low level spell.

[h2]Identify[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] The spell will have limits based upon the rarity of the item and the level of the caster as follows:
[ul]
[li]Uncommon items require a 3rd level caster
[li]Rare items require a 5th level caster
[li]Very Rare items require a 7th level caster
[li]Legendary items require a Legend Lore spell cast by a level 11+ spellcaster.
[/ul]
Casting Identify on an item above the caster's ability will provide a limited amount of information based on the gap. For example, a 1st level caster trying to identify a Very Rare item may only learn the item does posess magic and the nature/purpose/use of that magic. Anything more will require finding a higher level caster.

[b]Explanation:[/b] Allowing a 1st level caster be able to identify everything about a legendary item is too overpowered. And sometimes it's more fun to work for information.

[h1]Items[/h1]
[h2]Bag of Holding [/h2]
[b]Rule: [/b] Placing a bag of holding inside an extradimensional space created by a handy haversack, portable hole, or similar item instantly destroys both items and their contents but DOES NOT create a gate to the Astral Plane.

[b]Explanation:[/b] No "black hole bombs".


[h1]Miscellaneous[/h1]
[h2]Nat 1, Nat 20 on Saves/Skill Checks[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] Nat 1 is not an automatic fail and similarly, a Nat 20 is not an automatic success. A Nat 20 to seduce the dragon only works if the modified roll succeeds vs. the DC. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] Simplicity. 

[h2]Speaking of Seducing the Dragon[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] Success on a roll when trying to influence an NPC does not necessarily mean what you intended works exactly as you intended. It means a successful outcome; a Nat 20 would be the best possible outcome given the circumstances. 

For example, said dragon might chuckle politely and decide not to eat you. Similarly, a Nat 20 when trying to get the king to resign a make you the heir simply means you won't get sent to the executioner.

[b]Explanation:[/b] It's simply not that easy to influence people. For example, you are not going to convince someone who hates you to suddenly fall in love, at best you'll make them neutral. Multiple attempts over a period of time [b]may[/b] eventually get you to your goal.


[h2]Secrets, and when not to keep them[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] Don't keep secrets from the GM. Or from the other players unless there's a good reason. Keeping secrets from other characters is less of an issue. However, see Rule 1, above. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] If you're planning to surprise people or want to lead up to some sort of grand reveal and you don't make the GM aware, the story may not work out the way you want, and it will be spoiled. Similarly, not explaining an action you take to the GM may lead to innocent NPC actions that screw it up for you. 

[container:indent-40]
Example[sup]1[/sup]: Saying you're going to stay outside of the inn when everyone else goes in without explaining why. An NPC may see you spying through the window and ruin your big surprise entrance for roleplaying effects. If GM knows your plans, there's every likelihood you won't be seen. Similarly, having a long-range plan for a grand reveal to the party might not ever happen in the storyline veers away from what you had planned.

In both cases, the player could also let the other players in on the secret even if in a vague manner. The other characters of course know nothing. When the plan or reveal happens the other players will be better prepared to roleplay their characters' responses.
[/container]

[h2]We Aim to Please. You Aim Too Please[/h2]
[b]Rule:[/b] If you roll your dice and it misses the table (and lands on the floor) then you count as rolling the lowest possible result. Was it an attack roll? Counts as a 1. Rolling 2d6 damage and one die falls off the table? Then I guess you are doing d6+1 damage. 

[b]Explanation:[/b] D&D is an exciting game; however, lack of attention can ruin the experience for everybody such as rolling a die off the table and having to look for it. This stops the pacing of the game.
articles.footnotes: [sup]1[/sup] Both examples are real, and taken from D&D subreddits

articles.title: 4. Feats
articles.content:
A feat represents a talent or an area of expertise that gives a character special capabilities. It embodies training, experience, and abilities beyond what a class provides. At certain levels, your class gives you the Ability Score Improvement feature. Using the optional feats rule, you can forgo taking that feature to take a feat o f your choice instead. You can take each feat only once, unless the feat’s description says otherwise.

You must meet any prerequisite specified in a feat to take that feat. If you ever lose a feat’s prerequisite, you can’t use that feat until you regain the prerequisite. For example, the Grappler feat requires you to have a STR of 13 or higher. If your STR is reduced below 13 somehow—perhaps by a withering curse—you can’t benefit from the Grappler feat until your STR is restored.

[quote]
Any feat not formatted with [s]strikethrough[/s] is available if you meet the prerequisites
[/quote]

[h1]Player's Handbook[/h1]
[h2]Alert[/h2]
Always on the lookout for danger, you gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]You gain a +5 bonus to Initiative.
    [li]You can’t be surprised while you are conscious.
    [li]Other creatures don’t gain Advantage on attack rolls against you as a result of being hidden from you.
[/ul]

[h2]Athlete[/h2]
You have undergone extensive physical training to gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your STR or DEX score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]When you are prone, standing up uses only 5 feet of your movement.
    [li]Climbing doesn’t halve your speed.
    [li]You can make a running long jump or a running high jump after moving only 5 feet on foot, rather than 10 feet.
[/ul]

[h2]Actor[/h2]
Skilled at mimicry and dramatics, you gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your CHA score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You have Advantage on CHA (Deception) and CHA (Performance) checks when trying to pass yourself off as a different person.
    [li]You can mimic the speech of another person or the sounds made by other creatures. You must have heard the person speaking, or heard the creature make the sound, for at least 1 minute. A successful WIS (Insight) check contested by your CHA (Deception) check allows a listener to determine that the effect is faked.
[/ul]

[h2]Charger[/h2]
When you use your action to Dash, you can use a bonus action to make one melee weapon attack or to shove a creature. If you move at least 10 feet in a straight line immediately before taking this bonus action, you either gain a +5 bonus to the attack’s damage roll (if you chose to make a melee attack and hit) or push the target up to 10 feet away from you (if you chose to shove and you succeed).

[h2]Crossbow Expert[/h2]
Thanks to extensive practice with the crossbow, you gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]You ignore the loading quality of crossbows with which you are proficient.
    [li]Being within 5 feet of a hostile creature doesn’t impose Disdvantage on your ranged attack rolls.
    [li]When you use the Attack action and attack with a onehanded weapon, you can use a bonus action to attack with a loaded hand crossbow you are holding.
[/ul]

[h2]Defensive Duelist[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] DEX 13 or higher [br]
When you are wielding a finesse weapon with which you are proficient and another creature hits you with a melee attack, you can use your reaction to add your proficiency bonus to your AC for that attack, potentially causing the attack to miss you.

[h2]Dual Wielder[/h2]
You master fighting with two weapons, gaining the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]You gain a +1 bonus to AC while you are wielding a separate melee weapon in each hand.
    [li]You can use two-weapon fighting even when the onehanded melee weapons you are wielding aren’t light.
    [li]You can draw or stow two one-handed weapons when you would normally b e able to draw or stow only one.
[/ul]

[h2]Dungeon Delver[/h2]
Alert to the hidden traps and secret doors found in many dungeons, you gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]You have Advantage on WIS (Perception) and INT (Investigation) checks made to detect the presence of secret doors.
    [li]You have Advantage on saving throws made to avoid or resist traps.
    [li]You have resistance to the damage dealt by traps.
    [li]You can search for traps while traveling at a normal pace, instead of only at a slow pace.
[/ul]

[h2]Durable[/h2]
Hardy and resilient, you gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your CON score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]When you roll a Hit Die to regain hit points, the minimum number of hit points you regain from the roll equals twice your CON modifier (minimum of 2).
[/ul]

[h2]Elemental Adept[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] The ability to cast at least one spell [br]
When you gain this feat, choose one of the following damage types: acid, cold, fire, lightning, or thunder. Spells you cast ignore resistance to damage of the chosen type. In addition, when you roll damage for a spell you cast that deals damage of that type, you can treat any 1 on a damage die as a 2. You can select this feat multiple times. Each time you do so, you must choose a different damage type.

[h2]Grappler[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] STR 13 or higher [br]
You’ve developed the skills necessary to hold your own in close-quarters grappling. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]You have Advantage on attack rolls against a creature you are grappling.
    [li]You can use your action to try to pin a creature grappled by you. To do so, make another grapple check. If you succeed, you and the creature are both restrained until the grapple ends.
    [li]Creatures that are one size larger than you don’t automatically succeed on checks to escape your grapple.
[/ul]

[h2]Great Weapon Master[/h2]
You’ve learned to put the weight of a weapon to your Advantage, letting its momentum empower your strikes. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]On your turn, when you score a critical hit with a melee weapon or reduce a creature to 0 hit points with one, you can make one melee weapon attack as a bonus action.
    [li]Before you make a melee attack with a heavy weapon that you are proficient with, you can choose to take a -5 penalty to the attack roll. If the attack hits, you add +10 to the attack’s damage.
[/ul]

[h2]Healer[/h2]
You are an able physician, allowing you to mend wounds quickly and get your allies back in the fight. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]When you use a healer’s kit to stabilize a dying creature, that creature also regains 1 hit point.
    [li]As an action, you can spend one use of a healer’s kit to tend to a creature and restore 1d6+4 hit points to it, plus additional hit points equal to the creature’s maximum number of Hit Dice. The creature can’t regain hit points from this feat again until it finishes a short or long rest.
[/ul]

[h2]Heavily Armored[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] Proficiency with medium armor [br]
You have trained to master the use of heavy armor, gaining the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your STR score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You gain proficiency with heavy armor.
[/ul]

[h2]Heavy Armor Master[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] Proficiency with heavy armor [br]
You can use your armor to deflect strikes that would kill others. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your STR score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]While you are wearing heavy armor, bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage that you take from non magical weapons is reduced by 3.
[/ul]

[h2]Inspiring Leader[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] CHA 13 or higher [br]
You can spend 10 minutes inspiring your companions, shoring up their resolve to fight. When you do so, choose up to six friendly creatures (which can include yourself) within 30 feet of you who can see or hear you and w ho can understand you. Each creature can gain temporary hit points equal to your level plus your CHA modifier. A creature can’t gain temporary hit points from this feat again until it has finished a short or long rest.

[h2]Keen Mind[/h2]
You have a mind that can track time, direction, and detail with uncanny precision. You gain the following benefits.
[ul]
[li]Increase your INT score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You always know which way is north.
    [li]You always know the number of hours left before the next sunrise or sunset.
    [li]You can accurately recall anything you have seen or heard within the past month.
[/ul]

[h2]Lightly Armored[/h2]
You have trained to master the use of light armor, gaining the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your STR or DEX score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You gain proficiency with light armor.
[/ul]

[h2]Linguist[/h2]
You have studied languages and codes, gaining the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your INT score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You learn three languages of your choice.
    [li]You can ably create written ciphers. Others can’t decipher a code you create unless you teach them, they succeed on an INT check (DC equal to your INT score plus your proficiency bonus), or they use magic to decipher it.
[/ul]

[s][h2]Lucky[/h2]
You have inexplicable luck that seems to kick in at just the right moment. You have 3 luck points. Whenever you make an attack roll, an ability check, or a saving throw, you can spend one luck point to roll an additional d20. You can choose to spend one of your luck points after you roll the die, but before the outcome is determined. You choose which of the d20s is used for the attack roll, ability check, or saving throw. 

You can also spend one luck point when an attack roll is made against you. Roll a d20, and then choose whether the attack uses the attacker’s roll or yours. If more than one creature spends a luck point to influence the outcome of a roll, the points cancel each other out; no additional dice are rolled. 

You regain your expended luck points w hen you finish a long rest.[/s]

[h2]Mage Slayer[/h2]
You have practiced techniques useful in melee combat against spellcasters, gaining the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]When a creature within 5 feet of you casts a spell, you can use your reaction to make a melee weapon attack against that creature.
    [li]When you damage a creature that is concentrating on a spell, that creature has Disadvantage on the saving throw it makes to maintain its concentration.
    [li]You have Advantage on saving throws against spells cast by creatures within 5 feet of you.
[/ul]

[h2]Magic Initiate[/h2]
Choose a class: bard, cleric, druid, sorcerer, warlock, or wizard. You learn two cantrips of your choice from that class’s spell list. In addition, choose one 1st-level spell from that same list. You learn that spell and can cast it at its lowest level. Once you cast it, you must finish a long rest before you can cast it again. Your spellcasting ability for these spells depends on the class you chose: CHA for bard, sorcerer, or warlock; WIS for cleric or druid: or INT for wizard.

[h2]Martial Adept[/h2]
You have martial training that allows you to perform special combat maneuvers. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]You learn two maneuvers of your choice from among those available to the Battle Master archetype in the fighter class. If a maneuver you use requires your target to make a saving throw to resist the maneuver’s effects, the saving throw DC equals 8 plus your proficiency bonus plus your STR or DEX modifier (your choice).
    [li]If you already have superiority dice, you gain one more; otherwise, you have one superiority die, which is a d6. This die is used to fuel your maneuvers. A superiority die is expended when you use it. You regain your expended superiority d ice when you finish a short or long rest.
[/ul]

[h2]Medium Armor Master[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] Proficiency with medium armor [br]
You have practiced moving in medium armor to gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Wearing medium armor doesn’t impose Disadvantage on your DEX (Stealth) checks.
    [li]When you wear medium armor, you can add 3, rather than 2, to your AC if you have a DEX of
16 or higher.
[/ul]

[h2]Mobile[/h2]
You are exceptionally speedy and agile. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Your speed increases by 10 feet.
    [li]When you use the Dash action, difficult terrain doesn’t cost you extra movement on that turn.
    [li]When you make a melee attack against a creature, you don’t provoke opportunity attacks from that creature for the rest of the turn, whether you hit or not.
[/ul]

[h2]Moderately Armored[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] Proficiency with light armor [br]
You have trained to master the use of medium armor and shields, gaining the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your STR or DEX score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You gain proficiency with medium armor and shields.
[/ul]

[h2]Mounted Combatant[/h2]
You are a dangerous foe to face while mounted. While you are mounted and aren’t incapacitated, you gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]You have Advantage on melee attack rolls against any unmounted creature that is smaller than your mount.
    [li]You can force an attack targeted at your mount to target you instead.
    [li]If your mount is subjected to an effect that allows it to make a DEX saving throw to take only half damage, it instead takes no damage if it succeeds on the saving throw, and only half damage if it fails.
[/ul]

[h2]Observant[/h2]
Quick to notice details of your environment, you gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your INT or WIS score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]If you can see a creature’s mouth while it is speaking a language you understand, you can interpret what it’s saying by reading its lips.
    [li]You have a +5 bonus to your passive WIS (Perception) and passive INT (Investigation) scores.
[/ul]

[h2]Polearm Master[/h2]
You can keep your enemies at bay with reach weapons. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]When you take the Attack action and attack with only a glaive, halberd, or quarterstaff, you can use a bonus action to make a melee attack with the opposite end of the weapon. The weapon’s damage die for this attack is a d4, and the attack deals bludgeoning damage.
    [li]While you are wielding a glaive, halberd, pike, or quarterstaff, other creatures provoke an opportunity attack from you when they enter your reach.
[/ul]

[h2]Resilient[/h2]
Choose one ability score. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase the chosen ability score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You gain proficiency in saving throws using the chosen ability.
[/ul]

[h2]Ritual Caster[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] INT or WIS 13 or higher [br]
You have learned a number of spells that you can cast as rituals. 
These spells are written in a ritual book, which you must have in hand while casting one of them. When you choose this feat, you acquire a ritual book holding two 1st-level spells of your choice. Choose one of the following classes: bard, cleric, druid, sorcerer, warlock, or wizard. You must choose your spells from that class’s spell list, and the spells you choose must have the ritual tag. The class you choose also determines your spellcasting ability for these spells: CHA for bard, sorcerer, or warlock; WIS for cleric or druid; or INT for wizard. 

If you come across a spell in written form, such as a magical spell scroll or a wizard’s spellbook, you might be able to add it to your ritual book. The spell must be on the spell list for the class you chose, the spell’s level can be no higher than half your level (rounded up), and it must have the ritual tag. The process of copying the spell into your ritual book takes 2 hours per level of the spell, and costs 50 gp per level. The cost represents material components you expend as you experiment with the spell to master it, as well as the fine inks you need to record it.

[h2]Savage Attacker[/h2]
Once per turn when you roll damage for a melee weapon attack, you can reroll the weapon’s damage dice and use either total.

[h2]Sentinel[/h2]
You have mastered techniques to take Advantage of every drop in any enemy’s guard, gaining the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]When you hit a creature with an opportunity attack, the creature’s speed becomes 0 for the rest of the turn.
    [li]Creatures within 5 feet of you provoke opportunity attacks from you even if they take the Disengage action before leaving your reach.
    [li]When a creature within 5 feet of you makes an attack against a target other than you (and that target doesn’t have this feat), you can use your reaction to make a melee weapon attack against the attacking creature.
[/ul]

[h2]Sharpshooter[/h2]
You have mastered ranged weapons and can make shots that others find impossible. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Attacking at long range doesn't impose Disadvantage on your ranged weapon attack rolls.
    [li]Your ranged weapon attacks ignore half cover and three-quarters cover.
    [li]Before you make an attack with a ranged weapon that you are proficient with, you can choose to take a -5 penalty to the attack roll. If the attack hits, you add +10 to the attack’s damage.
[/ul]

[h2]Shield Master[/h2]
You use shields not just for protection but also for offense. You gain the following benefits while you are wielding a shield:
[ul]
[li]If you take the Attack action on your turn, you can use a bonus action to try to shove a creature within 5 feet of you with your shield.
    [li]If you aren’t incapacitated, you can add your shield’s AC bonus to any DEX saving throw you make against a spell or other harmful effect that targets only you.
    [li]If you are subjected to an effect that allows you to make a DEX saving throw to take only half damage, you can use your reaction to take no damage if you succeed on the saving throw, interposing your shield between yourself and the source of the effect.
[/ul]

[h2]Skilled[/h2]
You gain proficiency in any combination of three skills or tools of your choice.

[h2]Skulker[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] DEX 13 or higher [br]
You are expert at slinking through shadows. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]You can try to hide when you are lightly obscured from the creature from which you are hiding.
    [li]When you are hidden from a creature and miss it with a ranged weapon attack, making the attack doesn't reveal your position.
    [li]Dim light doesn’t impose Disadvantage on your WIS (Perception) checks relying on sight.
[/ul]

[h2]Spell Sniper[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] The ability to cast at least one spell [br]
You have learned techniques to enhance your attacks with certain kinds of spells, gaining the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]When you cast a spell that requires you to make an attack roll, the spell’s range is doubled.
    [li]Your ranged spell attacks ignore half cover and three-quarters cover.
    [li]You learn one cantrip that requires an attack roll. Choose the cantrip from the bard, cleric, druid, sorcerer, warlock, or wizard spell list. Your spellcasting ability for this cantrip depends on the spell list you chose from: CHA for bard, sorcerer, or warlock; WIS for cleric or druid; or INT for wizard.
[/ul]

[h2]Tavern Brawler[/h2]
Accustomed to rough-and-tumble fighting using whatever weapons happen to be at hand, you gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your STR or CON score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You are proficient with improvised weapons and unarmed strikes.
    [li]Your unarmed strike uses a d4 for damage.
    [li]When you hit a creature with an unarmed strike or an improvised weapon on your turn, you can use a bonus action to attempt to grapple the target.
[/ul]

[h2]Tough[/h2]
Your hit point maximum increases by an amount equal to twice your level when you gain this feat. Whenever you gain a level thereafter, your hit point maximum increases by an additional 2 hit points.

[h2]War Caster[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] The ability to cast at least one spell [br]
You have practiced casting spells in the midst of combat, learning techniques that grant you the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]You have Advantage on CON saving throws that you make to maintain your concentration on a spell when you take damage.
    [li]You can perform the somatic components of spells even when you have weapons or a shield in one or both hands.
    [li]When a hostile creature’s movement provokes an opportunity attack from you, you can use your reaction to cast a spell at the creature, rather than making an opportunity attack. The spell must have a casting time of 1 action and must target only that creature.
[/ul]

[h2]Weapon Master[/h2]
You have practiced extensively with a variety of weapons, gaining the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your STR or DEX score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You gain proficiency with four weapons of your choice.
[/ul]

[h1]Tasha’s Cauldron of Everything[/h1]
[s][h2]Artificer Initiate[/h2]
You've learned some of an artificer's inventiveness:
[ul]
[li]You learn one cantrip of your choice from the artificer spell list, and you learn one 1st-level spell of your choice from that list. INT is your spellcasting ability for these spells .
    [li]You can cast this feat's 1st-level spell without a spell slot, and you must finish a long rest before you can cast it in this way again. You can also cast the spell using any spell slots you have .
    [li]You gain proficiency with one type of artisan's tools of your choice, and you can use that type of tool as a spellcasting focus for any spell you cast that uses INT as its spellcasting ability.
[/ul][/s]

[h2]Chef[/h2]
Time spent mastering the culinary arts has paid off, granting you the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your CON or WIS score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You gain proficiency with cook's utensils if you don't already have it.
    [li]As part of a short rest, you can cook special food, provided you have ingredients and cook's utensils on hand. You can prepare enough of this food for a number of creatures equal to 4 plus your proficiency bonus. At the end of the short rest, any creature who eats the food and spends one or more Hit Dice to regain hit points regains an extra 1d8 hit points.
    [li]With one hour of work or when you finish a long rest, you can cook a number of treats equal to your proficiency bonus. These special treats last 8 hours after being made. A creature can use a bonus action to eat one of those treats to gain temporary hit points equal to your proficiency bonus.
[/ul]

[h2]Crusher[/h2]
You are practiced in the art of crushing your enemies, granting you the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your STR or CON by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]Once per turn, when you hit a creature with an attack that deals bludgeoning damage, you can move it 5 feet to an unoccupied space, provided the target is no more than one size larger than you .
    [li]When you score a critical hit that deals bludgeoning damage to a creature, attack rolls against that creature are made with Advantage until the start of your next turn.
[/ul]

[h2]Eldritch Adept[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Spellcasting or Pact Magic feature [br] Studying occult lore, you have unlocked eldritch power within yourself: you learn one Eldritch Invocation option of your choice from the warlock class. If the invocation has a prerequisite of any kind, you can choose that invocation only if you're a warlock who meets the prerequisite. Whenever you gain a level, you can replace the invocation with another one from the warlock class.

[h2]Fey Touched[/h2]
Your exposure to the Feywild's magic has changed you, granting you the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your INT, WIS, or CHA score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You learn the [i]Misty Step[/i] spell and one 1st-level spell of your choice. The 1st-level spell must be from the divination or enchantment school of magic. You can cast each of these spells without expending a spell slot. Once you cast either of these spells in this way, you can't cast that spell in this way again until you finish a long rest. You can also cast these spells using spell slots you have of the appropriate level. The spells' spellcasting ability is the ability increased by this feat.
[/ul]

[h2]Fighting Initiate[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Proficiency with a martial weapon [br] Your martial training has helped you develop a particular style of fighting. As a result, you learn one Fighting Style option of your choice from the fighter class. If you already have a style, the one you choose must be different. Whenever you reach a level that grants the Ability Score Improvement feature, you can replace this feat's fighting style with another one from the fighter class that you don't have.

[s][h2]Gunner[/h2]
You have a quick hand and keen eye when employing firearms, granting you the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your DEX score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You gain proficiency with firearms (see "Firearms" in the Dungeon Master's Guide).
    [li]You ignore the loading property of firearms.
    [li]Being within 5 feet of a hostile creature doesn't impose Disadvantage on your ranged attack rolls.
[/ul][/s]

[h2]Metamagic Adept[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]   Spellcasting or Pact Magic feature [br] You've learned how to exert your will on your spells to alter how they function:
[ul]
[li]You learn two Metamagic options of your choice from the Sorcerer class. You can use only one Metamagic option on a spell when you cast it, unless the option says otherwise. Whenever you reach a level that grants the Ability Score Improvement feature, you can replace one of these Metamagic options with another one from the sorcerer class.
    [li]You gain 2 sorcery points to spend on Metamagic (these points are added to any sorcery points you have from another source but can be used only on Metamagic). You regain all spent sorcery points when you finish a long rest.
[/ul]

[h2]Piercer[/h2]
You have achieved a penetrating precision in combat, granting you the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your STR or DEX by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]Once per turn, when you hit a creature with an attack that deals piercing damage, you can reroll one of the attack's damage dice, and you must use the new roll. When you score a critical hit that deals piercing damage to a creature, you can roll one additional damage die when determining the extra piercing damage the target takes.
[/ul]

[h2]Poisoner[/h2]
You can prepare and deliver deadly poisons, granting you the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]When you make a damage roll that deals poison damage, it ignores resistance to poison damage.
    [li]You can apply poison to a weapon or piece of ammunition as a bonus action, instead of an action.
    [li]You gain proficiency with the poisoner's kit if you don't already have it. With one hour of work using a poisoner's kit and expending 50 gp worth of materials, you can create a number of doses of potent poison equal to your proficiency bonus. Once applied to a weapon or piece of ammunition, the poison retains its potency for 1 minute or until you hit with the weapon or ammunition. When a creature takes damage from the coated weapon or ammunition, that creature must succeed on a DC 14 CON saving throw or take 2d8 poison damage and become poisoned until the end of your next turn .
[/ul]

[h2]Shadow Touched[/h2]
Your exposure to the Shadowfell's magic has changed you, granting you the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your INT, WIS, or CHA score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You learn the [i]Invisibility[/i] spell and one 1st-level spell of your choice. The 1st-level spell must be from the illusion or necromancy school of magic. You can cast each of these spells without expending a spell slot. Once you cast either of these spells in this way, you can't cast that spell in this way again until you finish a long rest. You can also cast these spells using spell slots you have of the appropriate level. The spells' spellcasting ability is the ability increased by this feat.
[/ul]

[h2]Skill Expert[/h2]
You have honed your proficiency with particular skills, granting you the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase one ability score of your choice by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You gain proficiency in one skill of your choice.
    [li]Choose one skill in which you have proficiency . You gain expertise with that skill, which means your proficiency bonus is doubled for any ability check you make with it. The skill you choose must be one that isn't already benefiting from a feature, such as Expertise, that doubles your proficiency bonus.
[/ul]

[h2]Slasher[/h2]
You've learned where to cut to have the greatest results, granting you the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your STR or DEX by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]Once per turn when you hit a creature with an attack that deals slashing damage, you can reduce the speed of the target by 10 feet until the start of you_r next turn.
    [li]When you score a critical hit that deals slashing damage to a creature, you grievously wound it. Until the start of your next turn, the target has Disadvantage on all attack rolls.
[/ul]

[h2]Telekinetic[/h2]
You learn to move things with your mind, granting you the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your INT, WIS, or CHA score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You learn the [i]Mage Hand[/i] cantrip. You can cast it without verbal or somatic components, and you can make the spectral hand invisible. If you already know this spell, its range increases by 30 feet when you cast it. Its spellcasting ability is the ability increased by this feat.
    [li]As a bonus action, you can try to telekinetically shove one creature you can see within 30 feet of you. When you do so, the target must succeed on a STR saving throw (DC 8 plus your proficiency bonus plus the ability modifier of the score increased by this feat) or be moved 5 feet toward you or away from you. A creature can willingly fail this save.
[/ul]

[h2]Telepathic[/h2]
You awaken the ability to mentally connect with others, granting you the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]Increase your INT, WIS, or CHA score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]You can speak telepathically to any creature you can see within 60 feet of you. Your telepathic utterances are in a language you know, and the creature understands you only if it knows that language. Your communication doesn't give the creature the ability to respond to you telepathically.
    [li]You can cast the [i]Detect Thoughts[/i] spell, requiring no spell slot or components, and you must finish a long rest before you can cast it this way again. Your spellcasting ability for the spell is the ability increased by this feat. If you have spell slots of 2nd level or higher, you can cast this spell with them.
[/ul]

[h1]Xanathar’s Guide to Everything[/h1]
[container:tablebox-40]
 [center] [b]Racial Feats[/b] [/center]
[table]
[tr][th]Race[/th][th]Feat[/th][/tr]
[tr][td]Dragonborn[/td][td]Dragon Fear[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Dragonborn[/td][td]Dragon Hide[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Dwarf[/td][td]Dwarven Fortitude[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Dwarf[/td][td]Squat Nimbleness[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Elf[/td][td]Elven Accuracy[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Elf (drow)[/td][td]Drow High Magic[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Elf (high)[/td][td]Fey Teleportation[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Elf (wood)[/td][td]Wood Elf Magic[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Gnome[/td][td]Fade Away[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Gnome[/td][td]Squat Nimbleness[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Half-elf[/td][td]Elven Accuracy[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Half-elf[/td][td]Prodigy[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Half-orc[/td][td]Orcish Fury[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Half-orc[/td][td]Prodigy[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Halfling[/td][td]Bountiful Luck[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Halfling[/td][td]Second Chance[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Halfling[/td][td]Squat Nimbleness[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Human[/td][td]Prodigy[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Tiefling[/td][td]Flames of Phlegethos[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Tiefling[/td][td]Infernal Constitution[/td][/tr]
[/table]
[/container]
These feats are each associated with a race from the [i]Player's Handbook[/i], as summarized in the Racial Feats table. A racial feat represents either a deepening connection to your race's culture or a physical trans formation that brings you closer to an aspect of your race's lineage.

The cause of a particular transformation is up to you and your DM. A transformational feat can symbolize a latent quality that has merged as you age, or a transformation might be the result of an event in the campaign, such as exposure to powerful magic or visiting a place of ancient significance to your race. Transformations a re a fundamental motif of fantasy literature and folklore.  Figuring out why your character has changed can be a r ich addition to your campaign's story.

[h2]Bountiful Luck[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] Halfling [br]
Your people have extraordinary luck, which you have Learned to mystically lend to your companions when you See them falter. You're not sure how you do it; you just Wish it, and it happens. Surely a sign of fortune's favor! When an ally you can see within 30 feet of you rolls a 1 on the d20 for an attack roll, an ability check, or a saving Throw, you can use your reaction to let the ally reroll the die. The ally must use the new roll. When you use this ability, you can't use your lucky Racial trait before the end of your next turn.

[h2]Dragon Fear[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Dragonborn [br]
When angered, you can radiate menace. You gain the Following benefits:
[ul]
[li]increase your STR, CON, or CHA Score by +1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]instead of exhaling destructive energy, you can expend A use of your breath weapon trait to roar, forcing Each creature of your choice within 30 feet of you to Make a WIS saving throw (DC 8 plus your proficiency Bonus plus your CHA modifier). A target automatically Succeeds on the save if it can't hear or see you. On a failed save, a target becomes frightened of you for 1 minute. If the frightened target takes any damage, it can repeat the saving throw, ending the effect On itself on a success.
[/ul]

[h2]Dragon Hide[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Dragonborn [br]
You manifest scales and claws reminiscent of your draconic Ancestors. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]increase your STR, CON, or CHA Score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]your scales harden. While you aren't wearing armor, you can calculate your AC as 13 plus your DEX modifier. You can use a shield and still gain this benefit.
    [li]you grow retractable claws from the tips of your fingers. Extending or retracting the claws requires no action. The claws are natural weapons, which you can use to make unarmed strikes. If you hit with them, you deal slashing damage equal to 1d4 plus your STR Modifier, instead of the normal bludgeoning damage for an unarmed s trike.
[/ul]

[h2]Drow High Magic[/h2][/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Elf (Drow) [br]
You learn more of the magic typical of dark elves. You Learn the detect magic spell and can cast it at will, without Expending a spell slot. You also learn levitate and Dispel magic, each of which you can cast once without Expending a spell slot. You regain the ability to cast Those two spells in this way when you finish a long rest. CHA is your spellcasting ability for all three spells.

[h2]Dwarven Fortitude[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Dwarf [br]
You have the blood of dwarf heroes flowing through Your veins. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]increase your CON score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]whenever you take the dodge action in combat, you can spend one hit die to heal yourself. Roll the die, add your CON modifier, and regain a number of hit points equal to the total (minimum of 1).
[/ul]

[h2]Elven Accuracy[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Elf or Half-Elf [br]
The accuracy of elves is legendary, especially that of elf Archers and spellcasters. You have uncanny aim with attacks That rely on precis ion rather than brute force. You Gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]increase your DEX, INT, WIS, or CHA Score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]whenever you have Advantage on an attack roll using DEX, INT, WIS, or CHA, you can reroll one of the dice once.
[/ul]

[h2]Fade away[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Gnome [br]
Your people are clever, with a knack for illusion magic. You have learned a magical trick for fading away when you suffer harm. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]increase your DEX or INT score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]immediately after you take damage, you can use a Reaction to magically become invisible until the end of your next turn or until you attack, deal damage, Or force someone to make a saving throw. Once you Use this ability, you can't do so again until you finish a short or long rest.
[/ul]

[h2]Fey Teleportation[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Elf (high) [br]
Your study of high elven lore has unlocked fey power that few other elves possess, except your eladrin cousins. Drawing on your fey ancestry, you can momentarily stride through the feywild to shorten your path from One place to another. You gain the following bene fits:
[ul]
[li]increase your INT or CHA score by 1, to A maximum of 20.
    [li]you learn to speak, read, and write sylvan.
    [li]you learn the misty step spell and can cast it once without expending a spell slot. You regain the ability to cast it in this way when you finish a short or long rest. INT is your spellcasting ability for this spell.
[/ul]

[h2]Flames of Phlegethos[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Tiefling [br]
You learn to call on hellfire to serve your commands. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]increase your INT or CHA score by 1, to A maximum of 20.
    [li]when you roll fire damage for a spell you cast, you can reroll any roll of 1 on the fire damage dice, but you must use the new roll, even if it is another 1.
    [li]whenever you cast a spell that deals fire damage, you can cause fames to wreathe you until the end of your next turn. The flames don't harm you or your possessions, and they shed bright light out to 30 feet and dim Light for an additional 30 feet. While the flames are present, any creature within 5 feet of you that hits you with a melee attack takes 1d4 fire damage.
[/ul]

[h2]Infernal CON[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Tiefiing [br]
Fiendish blood runs strong in you, unlocking a resilience akin to that possessed by some fiends. You gain the following benefits:
[ul]
[li]increase your CON score by 1, to a maximum of 20. 
    [li]you have resistance to cold dam age and poison Damage.
    [li]you have Advantage on saving throws against being Poisoned.
[/ul]

[h2]Orcish Fury[/h2] 
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] Half-Orc [br]
Your inner fury burns tirelessly. You gain the following Benefits:
[ul]
[li]increase your STR or CON score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]when you hit with an attack using a simple or martial weapon, you can roll one of the weapon's damage dice an additional time and add it as extra damage of the weapon's damage type. Once you use this ability, you can't use it again until you finish a short or long rest.
    [li]immediately after you use your Relentless Endurance trait, you can use your reaction to make one weapon attack.
[/ul]

[h2]Prodigy[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Half-Elf, Half-Orc, or Human [br]
You have a knack for learning new things. You gain the Following benefits:
[ul]
[li]you gain one s kill proficiency of your choice, one tool Proficiency of your choice, and fluency in one language of your choice.
    [li]choose one skill in which you have proficiency. You gain expertise with that skill, which means your proficiency bonus is doubled for any ability check you make with it. The skill you choose must be one that isn't already benefiting from a feature, such as expertise, that doubles your proficiency bonus.
[/ul]

[h2]Second Chance[/h2] 
[b]Prerequisite:[/b] Halfling [br]
Fortune favors you when someone tries to s trike you. You gain the following bene fits:
[ul]
[li]increase your DEX, CON, or CHA Score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]when a creature you can see hits you with an attack roll, you can use your reaction to force that creature to reroll. Once you use this ability, you can't use it again until you roll Initiative at the start of combat or until you finish a short or long rest.
[/ul]

[h2]Squat Nimbleness[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Dwarf or a small race [br]
You are uncommonly nimble for your race. You gain the Following benefits:
[ul]
[li]increase your STR or DEX score by 1, to a maximum of 20.
    [li]increase your walking speed by 5 feet.
    [li]you gain proficiency in the acrobatics or athletics skill (your choice). 
    [li]you have Advantage on any STR (athletics) or DEX (acrobatics) check you make to escape from being grappled.
[/ul]

[h2]Wood Elf Magic[/h2]
[b]Prerequisite:[/b]  Elf (Wood) [br]
You learn the magic of the primeval woods, which are revered and protected by your people. You learn one Druid cantrip of your choice. You also learn the [i]Longstrider[/i] And [i]pass without trace[/i] spells, each of which you can cast once without expending a spell slot. You regain the ability to cast these two spells in this way when you finish a long rest. WIS is your spellcasting ability for all three spells .

articles.title: Credits: The Free City of Haven
articles.content:
The city of Haven and it's contents are based on two modules originally published by Gamelords Ltd. in the early 1980s: [i]The Free City of Haven[/i], and [i]The Secrets of the Labyrinth[/i]. Sadly, the planned third module was never published, or at least I can find no record of it. 

In order to adapt the original work to my own purposes I have made a number of changes mainly consisting of the following:
- updating statblocks to 5e
- replacing orc NPCs with tieflings
- filling in some missing content on the North Corridor
- specific placement of individual shops, inns, etc.
- adjusting for some inconsistencies
- replacing the deities and religions


Due credit goes to the original authors of Haven...
- Kevin Hardwick, Kerry Lloyd,Richard Meyer, Larry Richardson, Janet Trautvetter, Michael Watkins, and Michael Welker;[br]
[br]
...and to the art and graphics creators:
- Wallace Miller, Dave Scalzi, Hannah M. G. Shapero, Larry Shade, Janet Trautvetter, Michael Welker, and V. M. Wyman.

articles.title: CT: Game within a Game
articles.content:
[h1]Chronogears Showdown[/h1]
[h2]Game Concept[/h2]
In this mini-game, players engage in a tabletop challenge where they compete against an in-game NPC or each other to simulate the process of creating and maintaining clockwork mechanisms. They use dice and cards to assemble their clockwork devices.

[h2]Materials Needed[/h2]
[ul]
[li]A set of polyhedral dice (d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, d20) for each player.
[li]A deck of standard playing cards.
[/ul]

[h2]How to Play[/h2]
Each player is dealt a hand of five cards from the deck. These cards represent clockwork parts, with the card value (Ace to King) representing the part's complexity and power. The suits could represent different types of parts: Hearts for energy sources, Diamonds for gears, Clubs for protective casings, and Spades for control mechanisms.

Each player then takes turns placing down a card, representing the installation of a part in their clockwork device. Players roll a D20 and must roll a number equal to or greater than the card value to successfully install the part (Ace is 1, Jack is 11, Queen is 12, King is 13).

If a player rolls less than the required value, they fail to install the part. The card is discarded, and the player must wait until their next turn to try again with a new card.

A player may choose to "upgrade" an existing part by playing a card of the same suit but higher value. They must then roll two dice:
the D20 to attempt the installation, as normal, and the D12. If the D12 roll is lower than the value of the existing card, the upgrade is successful. If not, both the existing card and the upgrade are discarded.

Once all players have played all their cards, the values of the successfully installed parts are totaled. The player with the highest total has built the most efficient clockwork device and wins the round. 

To add strategy to the game, consider adding special rules. For example, a player who places down a King can force another player to discard a card, or a player who places down an Ace can swap a card with another player.

This mini-game requires strategic thinking and a bit of luck, making it an engaging way to simulate the complexities of clockwork construction in a simplified form. It's also versatile and could be played virtually anywhere, as long as players have access to dice and a deck of cards.
